record_office_terminology.marriage_licenses: Probate Court (post-1911 statewide registration; pre-1911 found in deed books, church records)

burned_county_authority: FamilySearch Burned Counties Research wiki; SC Department of Archives and History county guides; Library of Congress South Carolina Courthouse Records guide (guides.loc.gov/south-carolina-local-history-genealogy/courthouse-records)
administrative_notes: South Carolina used a district system from 1785 to 1868. Pre-1868 records are often filed under district names (Camden, Cheraw, Colleton, Granville, Ninety-Six, Orangeburg, Pendleton, Richland, Williamsburg). The 1868 state constitution converted districts to counties. Researchers must check parent district records for pre-1868 ancestors.

core_repositories.source_name: South Carolina Department of Archives and History (SCDAH)
core_repositories.repository: SC Department of Archives and History
core_repositories.description: Primary state archive. Houses 350+ years of SC documents: land records, citizenship records, court records, military records, treasury records, General Assembly records. Over 3,000 cubic feet of manuscript county records. Research Room open Tue-Sat 8:30am-5pm.

core_repositories.repository: SC Department of Archives and History
core_repositories.description: Searchable database of 330,000+ online images and 375,000+ indexed names covering 13,000 places and 17,000 topics. Includes will transcripts (1782-1855, 21 counties), Confederate pension applications (1919-1938, 10,000+), plats for state land grants (1784-1868), legislative papers (1782-1866), criminal court records (1769-1891), school insurance photographs (1935-1952), and National Register properties.

core_repositories.repository: SC Department of Archives and History
core_repositories.description: Online catalog for searching and browsing the full holdings of SC Archives, including non-current state, county, and municipal records, plus copies of records from other government and private sources.
Diaries, papers, plantation records, business records, church records, maps, plats, genealogies, 30,000+ photographs, 50,000+ books/pamphlets/serials. Based in Charleston.

core_repositories.source_name: South Carolina State Library — Genealogy Resources
core_repositories.description: Genealogy subject guides including African American, Native American, and military resources. Research guides for using SC genealogical sources.

familysearch_collections.description: Extensive SC microfilm and digitized records. Includes county-level deed, will, marriage, probate, and court records. Many collections indexed (name-searchable); some browse-only. API access available for most record types.

familysearch_collections.source_name: FamilySearch — SC Online Genealogy Records Portal
familysearch_collections.description: Consolidated list of all online SC genealogy records available through FamilySearch, including direct links to digitized collections and external resources.

newspapers_and_periodicals.repository: Library of Congress / National Digital Newspaper Program
newspapers_and_periodicals.description: 125 South Carolina newspaper titles digitized and searchable, covering 1756-1963. Free full-text search. Part of the National Digital Newspaper Program.

newspapers_and_periodicals.repository: University of South Carolina Libraries / SC Digital Newspaper Program
newspapers_and_periodicals.description: Free access to historical local and regional newspapers from throughout South Carolina since 1815. Continuation of the SC Digital Newspaper Program (SCDNP) that began in 2009.

enslaved_persons_and_african_american.repository: SC Department of Archives and History
enslaved_persons_and_african_american.description: Slave census schedules (1850, 1860), free negro capitation tax books, manumission records, 1868 voter registration records, SC Land Commission records, and military records for African Americans.

enslaved_persons_and_african_american.source_name: American Origins — SC Enslaved Persons and Slaveholders Database
enslaved_persons_and_african_american.description: Ongoing project indexing names of enslaved persons and slaveholders from digitized Appraisals, Inventories and Sales Books in SC found on FamilySearch. Includes entries for freedmen and freedwomen.

enslaved_persons_and_african_american.source_name: FamilySearch — SC Freedmen Bureau Field Office Records
enslaved_persons_and_african_american.description: Bureau of Refugees, Freedmen, and Abandoned Lands records from SC field offices (1865-1872). Includes censuses, marriage records, medical records, labor contracts. Often includes full names, former slaveholders, plantation names, and current residences.
Slave records in the discrete manuscript collection at the College of Charleston. Includes plantation records, sales records, and other documents from the Lowcountry region.
Compiled genealogical data on SC slaveholders and enslaved persons, organized alphabetically. Focus on Lowcountry region but includes statewide references.

enslaved_persons_and_african_american.source_name: SC State Library — African American Genealogy Guide
enslaved_persons_and_african_american.description: Research guide to African American genealogy resources in South Carolina, including links to databases, archives, and research strategies.

native_american_and_removal_records.source_name: SC State Library — Native American Genealogy Guide
native_american_and_removal_records.description: Research guide for Native American genealogy in SC. The Catawba Indian Nation (Rock Hill, York County) is the only federally recognized tribe in SC. Other state-recognized groups include Beaver Creek, Edisto Natchez-Kusso, Pee Dee, Santee, Waccamaw, and Wassamaw tribes.
FamilySearch wiki page on SC indigenous peoples, including Catawba, Cherokee connections, and other tribal groups. Links to relevant record collections.

Key Catawba records include: Revolutionary paylist of 1780, Catawba petition of 1792, Catawba census of 1849, Massey's 1853 list of Catawbas, Catawba cemetery inscriptions, 1943 tribal roll. Headquarters in Rock Hill, York County. Primary western SC counties for Catawba research: York, Lancaster, Chester.
Eastern Cherokee census rolls covering 1835-1884. Relevant for SC Cherokee connections, particularly in upstate counties near NC border (Cherokee, Oconee, Pickens, Greenville).

municipal_and_city_records.repository: City of Charleston / SC Historical Society
municipal_and_city_records.description: Charleston, founded 1670, has extensive municipal records separate from county records. Merchant papers, port records, church records (St. Philip's, St. Michael's, French Huguenot, Kahal Kadosh Beth Elohim Jewish congregation). Among the richest colonial record sets in America.

municipal_and_city_records.source_name: College of Charleston — African American Genealogy LibGuide
municipal_and_city_records.description: Research guide focused on African American genealogy resources in the Charleston area, including links to Lowcountry Digital Library collections, plantation records, and local church records.

military_records.repository: SC Department of Archives and History
military_records.description: Revolutionary War muster rolls and service records, War of 1812 records, Mexican War records, Civil War Confederate military records including rosters and prisoner records. Available in Research Room; some digitized.

military_records.repository: SC Department of Archives and History
military_records.description: Over 10,000 digitized Confederate pension applications and transfer documents. From 1919-1925, SC granted pensions regardless of financial need. Applications include veteran name, residence, military unit/rank, wounds/disabilities, income/property, and application date. A 1923 law extended eligibility to African Americans who served 6+ months as cooks, servants, or attendants.

military_records.repository: SC Department of Archives and History
military_records.description: Indexed records of Confederate veterans in SC spanning 1909-1973. Searchable through the Online Records Index.

military_records.source_name: SC State Library — Military Records Genealogy Guide
military_records.description: Comprehensive guide to military genealogy resources for SC, covering Revolutionary War through modern conflicts. Links to federal and state-level military records.

land_and_property.repository: SC Department of Archives and History
land_and_property.description: 66 volumes (Charleston and Columbia series) with 51,055 digitized images of state plats from 1784-1868. Searchable by surname and placename. Critical resource for counties that lost antebellum records (Abbeville, Beaufort, Chesterfield, Colleton, Georgetown, Lexington, Orangeburg). Established by 1784 Act 'for Establishing the Mode and Conditions of Surveying and Granting the Vacant Lands Within this State.'
Microfilms of land grants recorded by the Surveyor General 1784-1882 (films 022531-580, index on film 022531). Original records at Secretary of State's Office, Columbia.

land_and_property.source_name: SCIway — County Register of Deeds Directory
land_and_property.description: Comprehensive directory of all 46 SC county Register of Deeds offices with links to online deed search portals where available. Coverage varies: some counties offer free images from 1980s-present; others offer indexes only.

vital_records.repository: SC Department of Archives and History
vital_records.description: Transcripts of wills from 21 SC counties, digitized and searchable through the Online Records Index. Coverage: 1782-1855.
Statewide marriage index for 1911-1969. Pre-1911 marriages were not centrally recorded; found in deed books, will books, church records, and Probate Court records.

vital_records.source_name: Library of Congress — SC Vital Records Guide
vital_records.description: LOC research guide for SC vital records. Notes that birth/death registration began statewide in 1915; delayed birth certificates were issued for pre-1915 births based on secondary sources (family bibles, baptismal records, witness testimony).

church_and_cemetery.repository: Various (SC Historical Society, SCDAH, individual churches)
church_and_cemetery.description: Charleston has extraordinarily rich colonial church records: St. Philip's Episcopal (1680s), St. Michael's Episcopal (1751), French Huguenot Church (1687), Kahal Kadosh Beth Elohim (1749, oldest Reform Jewish congregation in continuous use in US), First Baptist Church, First (Scots) Presbyterian, Circular Congregational. Many microfilmed by FamilySearch.

Microfilmed church records from various SC denominations including Methodist, Baptist, Presbyterian, Episcopal, Lutheran, and Quaker congregations. Check FamilySearch catalog by county for specific church holdings.

research_guides_and_societies.description: Statewide genealogical society with collection of family histories, genealogies, state and county record abstracts, county histories, local histories, and church histories.

research_guides_and_societies.source_name: Library of Congress — SC Local History & Genealogy Guide
research_guides_and_societies.description: LOC research guide covering SC courthouse records, vital records, and local history resources. Includes county-by-county notes on record survival and availability.

research_guides_and_societies.source_name: University of South Carolina — Genealogy Research Guide
research_guides_and_societies.description: USC library guide for genealogy research using South Caroliniana Library and other USC collections. Includes web resources and local archives.
200,000+ items from 40+ participating SC institutions. Includes local histories, maps, photographs, and documents. Free online access.

historical_counties_extinct.description: South Carolina used judicial districts from 1785 to 1868. The major districts were: Camden, Cheraw, Colleton, Granville, Ninety-Six, Orangeburg, Pendleton, Richland, and Williamsburg. In 1868, the new state constitution converted districts to counties. Many current counties were carved from these districts. Pre-1868 records are frequently filed under district names rather than the county names that later replaced them. No extinct counties exist in the modern sense — all 46 current counties are active.

burned_counties_summary.authority: FamilySearch Burned Counties Research wiki; LOC SC Courthouse Records guide; SCDAH county guides

counties.losses: Three courthouse fires destroyed virtually all records EXCEPT probate and equity court records. Deeds, court minutes, and other civil records lost.
Original courthouse burned. County formed 1919 so losses primarily affect 20th century records.

Federal soldiers attempted to fire courthouse; main structure saved but loose equity papers perished. Most probate records destroyed when Union cavalry intercepted officials attempting to move records to safety.
Advancing federal troops destroyed pre-1839 clerk of court records including deeds and virtually all probate records.
Public records had been removed to Columbia early in 1865 for safekeeping; ironically, they were burned there during Sherman's occupation on Feb 17, 1865.
Fire during federal occupation destroyed the courthouse and most of the records in it. Most severely affected county — pre-Civil War county records largely lost.

Fire destroyed the residence of Sumter District Clerk of Court John Horan in Stateburg, consuming probate records and deeds. This fire also destroyed records for Clarendon, Claremont, and Salem counties (predecessor jurisdictions).
